agent_output:
{
  "summary": {
    "verdict": "BLOCK",
    "one_liner": "Order Service directly queries Courier Service's database, creating tight coupling, race conditions, and data inconsistency — a textbook microservice anti-pattern that will break in production under any real load."
  },

  "critical_findings": [
    {
      "title": "Race condition: concurrent orders assign the same courier",
      "risk": "Two or more concurrent calls to assignCourierToOrder read the same 'available' courier row before either writes. Both orders get assigned the same courier. No locking, no optimistic concurrency, no compare-and-swap.",
      "severity": "Critical",
      "location": "order.service.ts:assignCourierToOrder — SELECT followed by UPDATE with no atomicity",
      "reproduction": "1. Courier C1 is the only available courier in zone X. 2. Order A and Order B both call assignCourierToOrder at the same time. 3. Both SELECT queries return C1. 4. Both UPDATE their own orders table with courierId = C1. 5. Courier C1 is now double-booked. Neither order fails.",
      "fix": "This must go through the Courier Service's API, which owns the assignment atomically (e.g., a single UPDATE ... SET status='assigned' WHERE status='available' RETURNING id, or a distributed lock). The Order Service should never perform this read-then-write across a boundary it doesn't own.",
      "time_to_fix": "4-8 hours (design API contract + implement in Courier Service)",
      "acceptance_criteria_affected": "Courier assignment correctness — one courier per order"
    },
    {
      "title": "Cross-database write inconsistency: courier status never updated",
      "risk": "The Order Service updates its own orders table (courierId, status='assigned') but never updates the courier's status in courier_db. The courier remains 'available' in the Courier Service, so subsequent orders will also select this courier — compounding the race condition into a guaranteed data divergence under any load.",
      "severity": "Critical",
      "location": "order.service.ts:assignCourierToOrder — only db.orders.update is called, courier_db is read-only",
      "reproduction": "1. Courier C1 is available. 2. Order A assigns C1 via db.orders.update. 3. C1's row in courier_db still says status='available'. 4. Order B reads courier_db, sees C1 available, assigns C1 again. 5. Repeat indefinitely — every order in this zone gets C1.",
      "fix": "Assignment must be owned by the Courier Service, which atomically marks the courier as unavailable and returns the assignment. If eventual consistency is acceptable, use an event (OrderAssigned) that the Courier Service consumes to update its own state — but the source of truth for courier availability must live in the Courier Service.",
      "time_to_fix": "4-8 hours (same redesign as finding 1)",
      "acceptance_criteria_affected": "Courier availability accuracy, order-to-courier mapping integrity"
    },
    {
      "title": "Direct cross-service database coupling breaks service autonomy",
      "risk": "The Order Service holds a direct connection pool to courier_db with hardcoded knowledge of the couriers table schema (column names, status enum values, zone column). Any Courier Service schema migration — rename, new status value, table restructure — silently breaks the Order Service with no compile-time or deploy-time warning. This also leaks credentials across service boundaries and makes independent deployability impossible.",
      "severity": "Critical",
      "location": "order.service.ts:1-10 — Pool connection to courier_db",
      "reproduction": "1. Courier team renames column 'zone' to 'delivery_zone' in a routine migration. 2. Courier Service deploys successfully — its own code is updated. 3. Order Service SELECT fails at runtime with 'column zone does not exist'. 4. All courier assignments fail. No test or CI catches this because the Order Service has no contract with the Courier Service schema.",
      "fix": "Replace the direct database connection with an HTTP/gRPC call to a Courier Service API endpoint (e.g., POST /couriers/assign { zone }). The Courier Service owns its data, exposes a versioned contract, and can evolve its schema independently. Remove courierDb Pool entirely.",
      "time_to_fix": "4-8 hours (define API, implement endpoint, replace caller)",
      "acceptance_criteria_affected": "Service independence, deployment safety, schema evolution"
    }
  ],

  "important_issues": [
    {
      "title": "No error handling or connection resilience on cross-service DB pool",
      "description": "The courierDb Pool has no connectionTimeoutMillis, idleTimeoutMillis, max connections, or retry logic. If the Courier DB is temporarily unreachable (network partition, failover), the Order Service hangs or crashes with an unhandled connection error. Pool is also created at module scope — a DNS resolution failure at import time takes down the entire service.",
      "location": "order.service.ts:4-9"
    },
    {
      "title": "Courier selection is non-deterministic and unfair",
      "description": "SELECT * ... rows[0] returns an arbitrary courier (database-dependent ordering). Under load, the same courier may be repeatedly selected while others idle. No load balancing, round-robin, or least-recently-assigned logic.",
      "location": "order.service.ts:16-21"
    }
  ],

  "suggestions": [
    "Introduce an idempotency key on courier assignment to make retries safe",
    "Add observability: emit metrics on assignment attempts, failures, and double-assignments so the race condition would be detected even if it shipped",
    "Consider an event-driven pattern (Order.Created → Courier.Assign) for decoupling if synchronous API latency is a concern"
  ],

  "production_readiness": {
    "deployment_risks": "Any schema change in courier_db will break this service silently at runtime. No contract or versioning exists between the two services.",
    "rollback_capability": "Rollback of Order Service is safe in isolation, but any orders assigned during the window will have inconsistent courier state (order says assigned, courier says available).",
    "backward_compatibility": "N/A — this is the current implementation, not a migration.",
    "monitoring_coverage": "No monitoring on courier assignment correctness. Double-bookings and stale-courier assignments will be silent.",
    "breaking_changes": {
      "api_breaking": false,
      "db_destructive": false,
      "shared_library": false,
      "auth_security": true,
      "data_pipeline": false,
      "config_changes": true,
      "details": [
        "Cross-service DB credentials (COURIER_DB_USER/PASSWORD) stored in Order Service env — security boundary violation",
        "Order Service requires network access to Courier DB host — infrastructure coupling"
      ]
    },
    "safe_to_deploy": false,
    "rollback_plan": "Revert Order Service deployment. Manually reconcile any orders assigned during the deploy window by cross-referencing order.courierId against courier.status in courier_db."
  },

  "escalations": [
    {
      "type": "architectural_concern",
      "description": "The Order Service directly connects to the Courier Service's database, violating service boundaries. The fix is not a code patch — it requires designing a proper inter-service communication pattern (synchronous API or async event) where the Courier Service owns courier assignment atomically. This is a foundational architecture decision that affects service contracts, deployment independence, and data ownership.",
      "routed_to": "MJ",
      "blocked_criteria": [
        "Courier assignment correctness under concurrent load",
        "Data consistency between order and courier state",
        "Independent deployability of Order and Courier services"
      ],
      "recommendation": "MJ should design the Courier Assignment API contract and decide sync (HTTP/gRPC) vs async (event-driven) assignment pattern. Until then, this code must not ship — it will double-book couriers under any real concurrency."
    }
  ],

  "confidence": {
    "level": 95,
    "high_confidence_areas": [
      "Race condition on courier assignment is trivially reproducible under concurrent requests",
      "Courier status is never updated — guaranteed data divergence",
      "Direct DB coupling is an unambiguous anti-pattern with well-understood failure modes"
    ],
    "low_confidence_areas": [],
    "assumptions": [
      "No external synchronization mechanism (distributed lock, queue) exists outside this code snippet",
      "The Courier Service does not have a separate process reconciling courier status"
    ]
  }
}

content.thinking:
Let me analyze this code carefully and produce my review.

This is a classic microservice anti-pattern: the Order Service is directly connecting to the Courier Service's database, creating tight coupling and multiple production risks.

Let me classify and form hypotheses quickly.

Classification: `architectural_concern` — the root cause is a cross-service database coupling that requires designing proper service boundaries (API calls, events, etc.).

Hypotheses:
1. Race condition: multiple order services read the same "available" courier simultaneously, both assign the same courier
2. No transaction across two databases — order gets updated but courier status never changes to "unavailable"


3. Schema changes in the courier database can silently break the order service since they're tightly coupled, and there's no connection pooling or error handling on this cross-service database link
